title: Interpret results of simulations
description: Practice estimating probabilities and making conclusions based on the results of a simulation.
topic_path: Get ready for probability and combinatorics

assessment_items.question_content:
Casey read a report that said the probability that a randomly selected American is left-handed is $14\%$. She was curious how many left-handed students to expect in a class of $30$ students.

She simulated $50$ classes of $30$ students where each student selected had a $0.14$ probability of being left-handed.

Casey counted how many left-handed students were in each simulated class. Here are her results:

![A dot plot for number of left-handed students has a scale from 0 to 8. The number of dots for each is as follows. 0, 1. 1, 5. 2, 12. 3, 13. 4, 14. 5, 2. 6, 2. 7, 1. 8, 0.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0ae0b3060ca5c0c53e6085e8735a6cb30ed87673)

**Use her results to estimate the probability that there are more than $5$ left-handed students in a class of $30$ students.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(\text{more than } 5 \text{ left-handed})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{\text{Three}}$ of the $50$ simulated classes had more than $5$ left-handed students.

![The previous dot plot for number of left-handed students is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 5 and 6, showing that  47 classes had 5 or less left-handed students and 3 had 6 or more.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a4ee566f6837b60cb8c1e4758325dccf9719b2cd)

The answer:

$P(\text{more than } 5 \text{ left-handed})\approx\dfrac{3}{50}= 0.06$

assessment_items.question_content:
Kaylee read a report that said the probability that a randomly selected American is left-handed is $14\%$. She was curious how many left-handed students to expect in a class of $25$ students.

She simulated $40$ classes of $25$ students where each student selected had a $0.14$ probability of being left-handed.

Kaylee counted how many left-handed students were in each simulated class. Here are her results:

![A dot plot for number of left-handed students has a scale from 0 to 8. The number of dots for each is as follows. 0, 1. 1, 0. 2, 7. 3, 13. 4, 11. 5, 5. 6, 2. 7, 0. 8, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1cf691cb8c2c5f0f318daa2ffd38e3976b782055)

**Use her results to estimate the probability that there are fewer than $3$ left-handed students in a class of $25$ students.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(\text{fewer than } 3 \text{ left-handed})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{\text{Eight}}$ of the $40$ simulated classes had fewer than $3$ left-handed students.

![The previous dot plot for number of left-handed students is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 2 and 3, showing that 8 trials had 2 or fewer left-handed students and 32 had 3 or more.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c6b2ec6fe0efa8e05d1f6e34dfb811ce9ccc9288)

The answer:

$P(\text{fewer than } 3 \text{ left-handed})\approx \dfrac{8}{40} =0.2$

assessment_items.question_content:
Javier read a report that said the probability that a randomly selected American is left-handed is $14\%$. He was curious how many left-handed students to expect in a class of $30$ students.

He simulated $100$ classes of $30$ students where each student selected had a $0.14$ probability of being left-handed.

Javier counted how many left-handed students were in each simulated class. Here are his results:

![A dot plot for number of left-handed students has a scale from 0 to 9. The number of dots for each is as follows. 0, 1. 1, 6. 2, 12. 3, 18. 4, 27. 5, 19. 6, 12. 7, 2. 8, 2. 9, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/207761d9e4bb9b44a762d567e120bef89d7e572d)

**Use his results to estimate the probability that there are $7$ or more left-handed students in a class of $30$ students.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(7\text{ or more left-handed})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{\text{Five}}$ of the $100$ simulated classes had $7$ or more left-handed students.

![The previous dot plot for number of left-handed students has a dashed vertical line drawn between 6 and 7, showing that 95 classes had 6 or fewer left-handed students and 5 had 7 or more.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a55264564449a6606553cad17a1cc3b449cf8bc4)

The answer:

$P(7\text{ or more left-handed})\approx \dfrac{5}{100} =0.05$

assessment_items.question_content:
Chayton read a report that said the probability that a randomly selected American is left-handed is $14\%$. He was curious how many left-handed students to expect in a class of $20$ students.

He simulated $25$ classes of $20$ students where each student selected had a $0.14$ probability of being left-handed.

Chayton counted how many left-handed students were in each simulated class. Here are his results:

![A dot plot for number of left-handed students has a scale from 0 to 5. The number of dots for each is as follows. 0, 0. 1, 4. 2, 5. 3, 11. 4, 4. 5, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/73256391ee37956756e37f761a3a8acd2f71674a)

**Use his results to estimate the probability that there are $2$ or fewer left-handed students in a class of $20$ students.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(2\text{ or fewer left-handed})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{\text{Nine}}$ of the $25$ simulated classes had $2$ or fewer left-handed students.

![The previous dot plot of left-handed students is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 2 and 3, showing that 9 classes had 2 or fewer left-handed students and 16 had more than 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/25353fef60e05d0dc2629b227443e24b5bc3d0c6)

The answer:

$P(2\text{ or fewer left-handed})\approx \dfrac{9}{25} =0.36$

assessment_items.question_content:
The probability that Maria succeeds at any given free-throw is $75\%$. She was curious how many free-throws she can expect to succeed in a sample of $10$ free-throws.

She simulated $25$ samples of $10$ free-throws where each free-throw had a $0.75$ probability of being a success.

Maria counted how many free-throws were successes in each simulated sample. Here are her results:

![A dot plot for number of free-throw success has a scale from 5 to 10. The number of dots for each is as follows. 5, 0. 6, 5. 7, 8. 8, 6. 9, 5. 10, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/761109e578d3485c5004a03bbf5c78d56e192b33)

**Use her results to estimate the probability that she succeeds at fewer than $7$ free-throws in a sample of $10$ free-throws.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(\text{fewer than } 7 \text{ successes})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{\text{Five}}$ of the $25$ simulated samples had fewer than $7$ successful free-throws.

![The previous dot plot for number of free-throw successes is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 6 and 7, showing that in 5 samples had less than 7 successful free-throws and 20 had 7 or more.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4ee84cc333d5166d05dd4c457147659a5cacca4f)

The answer:

$P(\text{fewer than } 7 \text{ successes})\approx\dfrac{5}{25}=\dfrac15=0.2$

assessment_items.question_content:
The probability that Naoya succeeds at any given free-throw is $70\%$. He was curious how many free-throws he can expect to succeed in a sample of $15$ free-throws.

He simulated $20$ samples of $15$ free-throws where each free-throw had a $0.7$ probability of being successful.

Naoya counted how many free-throws were successful in each simulated sample. Here are his results:

![A dot plot for number of free throw success has a scale from 8 to 14. The number of dots for each is as follows. 8, 1. 9, 3. 10, 3. 11, 5. 12, 5. 13, 2. 14, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/436b93586655b9bd3995ad9aac72f5eb6357ec5a)

**Use his results to estimate the probability that he succeeds at greater than $10$ free-throws in a sample of $15$ free-throws.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(\text{greater than } 10 \text{ successes})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
In $\blueD{7}$ of the $20$ simulated trials, Naoya counted up to $10$ successes.

So in $\purpleD{\text{thirteen}}$ samples had greater than $10$ successful free-throws.

![The previous dot plot of free throw success is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 10 and 11, showing that 7 samples had 10 or fewer successful free throws and 13 samples had more than 10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/edf6ff5c7be712006fa289555dcf7d6ae217844d)

The answer:

$P(\text{greater than } 10 \text{ successes})\approx\dfrac{13}{20}=0.65$

assessment_items.question_content:
The probability that Rahul succeeds at any given free-throw is $70\%$. He was curious how many free-throws he can expect to succeed in a sample of $15$ free-throws.

He simulated $30$ samples of $15$ free-throws where each free-throw had a $0.7$ probability of being successful.

Rahul counted how many free-throws were successful in each simulated sample. Here are his results:

![A dot plot for number of free throw success has a scale from 10 to 15. The number of dots for each is as follows. 10, 1. 11, 5. 12, 8. 13, 9. 14, 6. 15, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/40b67e1733dd08242a2dac3056d4dcc65d699d9b)

**Use his results to estimate the probability that he succeeds at $11$ or fewer free-throws in a sample of $15$ free-throws.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(11 \text{ or fewer successes})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{\text{Six}}$ of the $30$ simulated samples had $11$ or fewer successful free-throws.

![The previous dot plot of free throw success is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 11 and 12, showing that 6 samples had 11 or fewer successful free throws and 24 samples had more than 11.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1c103b9e7e2930c20f05529f3045da5b69f14523)

The answer:

$P(11 \text{ or fewer successes})\approx\dfrac{6}{30} =\dfrac{1}{5}=0.2$

assessment_items.question_content:
The probability that Shruti succeeds at any given free-throw is $80\%$. She was curious how many free-throws she can expect to succeed in a sample of $12$ free-throws.

She simulated $25$ samples of $12$ free-throws where each free-throw had a $0.8$ probability of being a success.

Shruti counted how many free-throws were successes in each simulated sample. Here are her results:

![A dot plot for number of free-throw successes has a scale from 8 to 12. The number of dots for each is as follows. 8, 1. 9, 4. 10, 9. 11, 9. 12, 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d943414a69585c3e53f87a36514deb4ab659d9a1)

**Use her results to estimate the probability that she succeeds at $10$ or more free-throws in a sample of $12$ free-throws.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(10 \text{ or more successes})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
In $\blueD{5}$ of the $25$ simulated trials, Shruti counted fewer than $10$ successes.

So $\purpleD{\text{twenty}}$ samples had $10$ or more successful free-throws.

![The previous dot plot for number of free-throw successes is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 9 and 10, showing that 5 trials had less than 10 successes and 20 had 10 or more.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ba59420bc71792eded597a79c3f5ae87c6cab0d3)

The answer:

$P(10 \text{ or more successes})\approx\dfrac{20}{25}=\dfrac45=0.8$

assessment_items.question_content:
A cereal company is putting $1$ of $3$ prizes in each box of cereal. The prizes are evenly distributed so the probability of winning any given prize is always $1/3$.

Adam wonders how many boxes he should expect to buy to get all $3$ prizes. He carried out $32$ trials of a simulation and his results are shown below. Each dot represents how many boxes it took to get all $3$ prizes in that trial.

![A dot plot for number of boxes purchased has a scale from 3 to 13. The number of dots for each is as follows. 3, 8. 4, 7. 5, 5. 6, 3. 7, 3. 8, 2. 9, 1. 10, 1. 11, 1. 12, 0. 13, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9bfbba58a0163debc4a5d30ad2cc73224533e495)

**Use his results to estimate the probability that it takes $9$ or more boxes to get all $3$ prizes.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(9 \text{ or more boxes})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
It took $9$ or more boxes in $\purpleD{4}$ of the $32$ simulated trials.

![The previous dot plot for number of boxes purchased is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 8 and 9, showing that 28 trials took 8 or fewer boxes and 4 took 9 or more.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/331f1a1653e5a7fd88a64254187515e74971d463)

The answer:

$P(9 \text{ or more boxes})\approx\dfrac{4}{32} =\dfrac18=0.125$

assessment_items.question_content:
A cereal company is putting $1$ of $3$ prizes in each box of cereal. The prizes are evenly distributed so the probability of winning any given prize is always $1/3$.

Mohamed wonders how many boxes he should expect to buy to get all $3$ prizes. He carried out $24$ trials of a simulation and his results are shown below. Each dot represents how many boxes it took to get all $3$ prizes in that trial.

![A dot plot for number of boxes purchased has a scale from 3 to 14. The number of dots for each is as follows. 3, 9. 4, 3. 5, 4. 6, 4. 7, 0. 8, 0. 9, 1. 10, 2. 11, 0. 12, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4ca231089721ae1879382e54a2d9ad098e61b514)

**Use his results to estimate the probability that it takes more than $3$ boxes to get all $3$ prizes.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(\text{more than }3 \text{ boxes})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
In $\blueD{9}$ of the $24$ simulated trials, it took up to $3$ boxes.

So in the other $\purpleD{15}$ trials, it took more than $3$ boxes.

![The previous dot plot for number of boxes purchased is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 3 and 4, showing that 9 trials too up to 3 boxes and 15 took more than 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cbe828eabea4cb5fa695fcf13bd54683188904aa)

The answer:

$P(\text{more than }3 \text{ boxes})\approx\dfrac{15}{24} =\dfrac{5}{8}=0.625$

assessment_items.question_content:
A cereal company is putting $1$ of $4$ prizes in each box of cereal. The prizes are evenly distributed so the probability of winning any given prize is always $1/4$.

Vera wonders how many boxes she should expect to buy to get all $4$ prizes. She carried out $36$ trials of a simulation and her results are shown below. Each dot represents how many boxes it took to get all $4$ prizes in that trial.

![A dot plot for number of boxes purchased has a scale from 4 to 15. The number of dots drawn for each is as follows. 4, 4. 5, 6. 6, 3. 7, 4. 8, 4. 9, 6. 10, 3. 11, 2. 12, 1. 13, 2. 14, 0. 15, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a264ee971baf4a4cb95a7cb0edd6c35783dc734e)

**Use her results to estimate the probability that it takes $9$ or fewer boxes to get all $4$ prizes.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(9 \text{ or fewer boxes})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
In $\blueD{9}$ of the $36$ simulated trials, it took more than $9$ boxes.

So in the other $\purpleD{27}$ trials, it took $9$ or fewer boxes.

![The previous dot plot for number of boxes purchased is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 9 and 10, showing that 27 trials took 9 or fewer boxes and 9 took more than 9.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9905a63f9ab9f043cad410056c5cbce72ab74f39)

The answer:

$P(9 \text{ or fewer boxes})\approx\dfrac{27}{36} =\dfrac{3}{4}=0.75$

assessment_items.question_content:
A cereal company is putting $1$ of $4$ prizes in each box of cereal. The prizes are evenly distributed so the probability of winning any given prize is always $1/4$.

Lily wonders how many boxes she should expect to buy to get all $4$ prizes. She carried out $20$ trials of a simulation and her results are shown below. Each dot represents how many boxes it took to get all $4$ prizes in that trial.

![A dot plot for number of boxes purchased has a scale from 4 to 15. The number of dots for each is as follows. 4, 2. 5, 3. 6, 1. 7, 3. 8, 2. 9, 5. 10, 1. 11, 1. 12, 1. 13, 0. 14, 0. 15, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/820537577d8bd2bc26940eb3ec12a312da5c2566)

**Use her results to estimate the probability that it takes fewer than $7$ boxes to get all $4$ prizes.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(\text{fewer than }7 \text{ boxes})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
It took fewer than $7$ boxes in $\purpleD{6}$ of the $20$ simulated trials.

![The previous dot plot for number of boxes purchased is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 6 and 7, showing that 6 trials took 6 or fewer boxes and 14 took 7 or more.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c2c38e98863262c69a8b921d14e4c28b4f488547)

The answer:

$P(\text{fewer than }7 \text{ boxes})\approx\dfrac{6}{20} = \dfrac{3}{10}=0.3$

assessment_items.question_content:
The probability that Gerald makes a three-point shot in basketball is $20\%$. For practice, Gerald will regularly shoot a series of these shots until he succeeds at one. He's curious how many shots it will typically take him to get his first successful shot.

He simulated $40$ trials of three-point shots where each shot had a $0.2$ probability of being successful, and in each trial, he counted how many  shots it took to get the first successful shot.
 Here are his results:

![A dot plot for number of shots taken has a scale from 0 to 16. The number of dots for each is as follows. 0, 0. 1, 9. 2, 6. 3, 5. 4, 4. 5, 4. 6, 2. 7, 2. 8, 0. 9, 1. 10, 1. 11, 1. 12, 2. 13, 1. 14, 0. 15, 1. 16, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fd71a0853336cd12080bcf30639c505a517eb003)

**Use his results to estimate the probability that it takes $10$ or fewer shots to get his first successful shot.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(10 \text{ or fewer shots})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
In $\blueD{6}$ of the $40$ simulated trials, it took more than $10$ shots to get his first successful shot.

So in the other $\purpleD{34}$ trials, it took $10$ or fewer shots to get his first successful shot.

![The previous dot plot for number of shots taken is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 10 and 11, showing that in 34 trials it took 10 or fewer shots and in 6 it took 11 or more.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ee7de1474e8fb3b891ce59643d4f494aac99349a)

The answer:

$P(10 \text{ or fewer shots})\approx\dfrac{34}{40}=\dfrac{17}{20}=0.85$

assessment_items.question_content:
The probability that Liu Yang makes a three-point shot in basketball is $10\%$. For practice, Liu Yang will regularly shoot a series of these shots until she makes one. She's curious how many shots it will typically take her to get her first successful shot.

She simulated $30$ trials of three-point shots where each shot had a $0.1$ probability of being made, and in each trial, she counted how many  shots it took to get the first successful shot.
 Here are her results:

![A dot plot for number of shots taken has a scale from 0 to 28. The number of dots for each is as follows. 0, 0. 1, 6. 2, 4. 3, 1. 4, 0. 5, 2. 6, 2. 7, 1. 8, 3. 9, 0. 10, 2. 11, 1. 12, 1. 13, 1. 14, 0. 15, 0. 16, 0. 17, 1. 18, 1. 19, 0. 20, 1. 21, 0. 22, 0. 23, 1. 24, 0. 25, 0. 26, 1. 27, 0. 28, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/32468823b5623d825496362df3b66aa0d26b7489)

**Use her results to estimate the probability that it takes $11$ or more shots to get her first successful shot.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(11 \text{ or more shots})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
In $\purpleD{9}$ of the $30$ simulated trials, it took $11$ or more shots to get her first successful shot.

![The previous dot plot for number of shots taken is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 10 and 11, showing that in 21 trials it took 10 or fewer shots and in 9 it took 11 or more.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ce7e2b2a4d671887ba14df733244523c11a6a6de)

The answer:

$P(11 \text{ or more shots})\approx\dfrac{9}{30}=\dfrac{3}{10}=0.3$

assessment_items.question_content:
The probability that Alma makes a three-point shot in basketball is $20\%$. For practice, Alma will regularly shoot a series of these shots until she makes one. She's curious how many shots it will typically take her to get her first successful shot.

She simulated $50$ trials of three-point shots where each shot had a $0.2$ probability of being made, and in each trial, she counted how many  shots it took to get the first successful shot.
 Here are her results:

![A dot plot for number of shots taken has a scale from 0 to 26. The number of dots for each is as follows. 0, 0. 1, 11. 2, 10. 3, 5. 4, 5. 5, 5. 6, 2. 7, 2. 8, 3. 9, 0. 10, 2. 11, 0. 12, 0. 13, 2. 14, 1. 15, 0. 16, 0. 17, 1. 18, 0. 19, 0. 20, 0. 21, 0. 22, 0. 23, 0. 24, 0. 25, 0. 26, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7f3d5c0039711ba44974c02be43f0f44e22daab8)

**Use her results to estimate the probability that it takes fewer than $7$ shots to get her first successful shot.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(\text{fewer than }7 \text{ shots})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
In $\blueD{12}$ of the $50$ simulated trials, it took $7$ or more shots to get her first successful shot.

So in the other $\purpleD{38}$ trials, it took fewer than $7$ shots to get her first successful shot.

![The previous dot plot for number of shots taken is displayed. A dashed vertical line is drawn between 6 and 7, showing that 38 trials took 6 or fewer shots and 12 took 7 or more.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eae7576b1a46716d74e11ce07b4df9b64654e4f5)

The answer:

$P(\text{fewer than }7 \text{ shots})\approx \dfrac{38}{50}=0.76$

assessment_items.question_content:
The probability that Leon makes a three-point shot in basketball is $15\%$. For practice, Leon will regularly shoot a series of these shots until he makes one. He's curious how many shots it will typically take him to get his first successful shot.

He simulated $25$ trials of three-point shots where each shot had a $0.15$ probability of being made, and in each trial, he counted how many  shots it took to get the first successful shot.
 Here are his results:

![A dotplot has a horizontal axis labeled, Number of shots taken, which ranges from 0 to 26, in increments of 1. The number of dots above each value is as follows: 1, 5; 2, 3; 3,  1; 4, 1; 5, 2; 6, 1; 7, 2; 8, 2; 10, 1; 11, 1; 17, 2; 18, 1; 20, 1; 23, 1, 26, 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5f42ff0c758434583e65b3dc602fd392faba934d)

**Use his results to estimate the probability that it takes more than $5$ shots to get his first successful shot.**  
*Give your answer as either a fraction or a decimal.*

$P(\text{more than }5 \text{ shots})\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
In $\blueD{12}$ of the $25$ simulated trials, it took $5$ or fewer shots to get his first successful shot.

So in the other $\purpleD{13}$ trials, it took more than $5$ shots to get his first successful shot.

![A dotplot has a horizontal axis labeled, Number of shots taken, which ranges from 0 to 26, in increments of 1. The number of dots above each value is as follows: 1, 5; 2, 3; 3,  1; 4, 1; 5, 2; 6, 1; 7, 2; 8, 2; 10, 1; 11, 1; 17, 2; 18, 1; 20, 1; 23, 1, 26, 1. A dashed vertical line rises from the horizontal axis at 5. All points plotted rightward of the line are shaded purple.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/84746d7e92f105ff7ed2e3cd4976b7fb2eec0e33)

The answer:

$P(\text{more than }5 \text{ shots})\approx \dfrac{13}{25}=0.52$
